era_context: The Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I is consolidating its control and establishing itself as a major imperial power in western Asia. The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces mounting military and political pressures. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is well established, with Wei, Wu, and Shu competing for dominance. This is a year of continued consolidation and military competition across multiple empires.

events.title: Sassanid military campaigns in Mesopotamia and Syria
events.description: Ardashir I continues military campaigns against Roman-held territories in Mesopotamia and Syria. These campaigns are part of Ardashir's broader strategy to reclaim former Achaemenid territories and establish Sassanid hegemony in western Asia. The campaigns are partially successful, with Sassanid forces capturing some Roman-held cities and territories. Roman Emperor Severus Alexander prepares a military response, but the campaign is delayed by internal political instability.
events.certainty_note: Sassanid military campaigns in this period are well attested, but precise dating of individual campaigns varies by source.
cross_references: Part of broader Sassanid expansion (224-240 CE)

events.title: Wei-Wu military conflict in Three Kingdoms period
events.description: Military conflict between the states of Wei and Wu continues as part of the ongoing Three Kingdoms struggle. Cao Pi's Wei forces engage with Sun Quan's Wu forces in battles for control of central China. The conflict is characterized by naval warfare on the Yangtze River and land campaigns in the border regions. Neither side achieves decisive victory, but the conflict establishes the military balance that will characterize the Three Kingdoms period.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: Wei-Wu military conflict during this period is well documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)

events.title: Shu Han consolidation under Liu Bei and Zhuge Liang
events.description: Liu Bei and his strategist Zhuge Liang continue consolidating control over southwestern China. Shu Han develops administrative structures and military organization to compete with Wei and Wu. The state's economy is based on agricultural production in the Sichuan basin and control of trade routes. Zhuge Liang's administrative reforms strengthen Shu Han's capacity for sustained competition in the Three Kingdoms struggle.
events.certainty_note: Shu Han's consolidation is well documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)

events.title: Roman political instability and succession disputes
events.description: Severus Alexander's reign continues to be marked by political instability and succession disputes. The emperor's mother, Julia Maesa, wields significant influence over imperial policy. The military becomes increasingly factional and politicized, with different units supporting different factions. The imperial government struggles to maintain coherent policy in the face of military pressures on multiple frontiers and internal political conflict.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The general political instability of Severus Alexander's reign is well attested, though specific events in 222 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman political crisis (220-235 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of individual Sassanid military campaigns in the early 220s CE is disputed among scholars. Some sources place major campaigns in 224-225 CE rather than 222 CE. The Historia Augusta's account of Severus Alexander's reign contains legendary material and is considered unreliable by modern scholars. The exact chronology of Wei-Wu military conflicts is not always clear in surviving sources, and specific battles in 222 CE may be conflated with events in surrounding years.
historiographic_note: 222 CE is a year of continued military and political competition across multiple empires, but documentation is heavily dependent on later sources. Roman history relies on the Historia Augusta and later historians, which are of variable reliability. Chinese history is well documented through the Sanguozhi and later compilations. Sassanid history is known primarily through later Persian sources and Roman accounts. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Part of broader Sassanid military expansion
Wei-Wu conflict and Shu Han consolidation were concurrent in Three Kingdoms period
Political instability contributed to Severus Alexander's assassination
